title: Research
text:
My research focuses on understanding human relationships and sexual behavior from an evolutionary and behavioral genetics perspective.




Investigating how family environments influence sexual development and mating strategies.

title: Research
text: Investigating how family environments influence sexual development and mating strategies.

title: Code
text:
codechest is an R package I began developing simply because I found myself repeatedly writing similar functions across different research projects. Over time, I started collecting and refining these functions so that I could move more efficiently between projects and maintain more consistent workflows.
The package is therefore best thought of as a personal toolbox of reusable functions for data cleaning, statistical modeling, and research workflows. I continue to expand and update the package as I encounter new problems that benefit from reusable solutions.
The code is still evolving and includes some quirks and idiosyncrasies that reflect the projects it grew out of. However, many of the functions may still be useful for others working on similar problems, and researchers are welcome to explore and adapt them.
Repository: github.com/ryandobson/codechest


You can install the development version of codechest from GitHub using:
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("ryandobson/codechest")






pub_cors_all() quickly generates a publication-ready correlation table for a set of variables.

#grab specific variable names as a vector
ll_aos <- names(ll[, grepl("rec_aos", colnames(ll))])

#create a vector to display names nicely (optional)
nice_names <- c(
  "oral sex"        = "rec_aos_pt_1",
  "pleasing sexual" = "rec_aos_pt_2"
)

pub_cors_all(data       = ll, #dataset 
             cor_vars   = ll_aos,  #vector of variabl names from the dataset to include in analysis
             rename_vec = nice_names, #named vector to map new variable names
             cor_use    = "pairwise", #defaults to "pairwise" deletion of cases
             rnd        = 2 #default rounds to 2
             )

                       1     2    3     4    5    6    7    8
oral sex (1)        1.00  0.71 0.23  0.01 0.01 0.07 0.04 0.01
pleasing sexual (2) 0.71  1.00 0.27 -0.02 0.02 0.06 0.02 0.05
rec_aos_pt_3 (3)    0.23  0.27 1.00  0.29 0.27 0.27 0.27 0.24
rec_aos_1 (4)       0.01 -0.02 0.29  1.00 0.46 0.37 0.39 0.32
rec_aos_2 (5)       0.01  0.02 0.27  0.46 1.00 0.36 0.38 0.33
rec_aos_3 (6)       0.07  0.06 0.27  0.37 0.36 1.00 0.61 0.47
rec_aos_4 (7)       0.04  0.02 0.27  0.39 0.38 0.61 1.00 0.57
rec_aos_5 (8)       0.01  0.05 0.24  0.32 0.33 0.47 0.57 1.00


pub_cors_by() is an extension of pub_cors_all() that takes a dichotomous grouping variable and displays correlations for one group above the diagonal, and the other below.
I have updates to this function that I haven’t incorporated into my package yet which includes: - automatic calculation of the difference between grouping variable calculations (helps quickly identify interaction effects in correlations)

pub_cors_by(data        = ll, #dataset 
            cor_vars    = ll_aos,  #vector of variabl names from the dataset to include in analysis
            group_var   = "sex_bio",
            groups      = levels(ll$sex_bio),
            rename_vec  = nice_names, #named vector to map new variable names
            cor_use     = "pairwise", #defaults to "pairwise" deletion of cases
            rnd         = 2 #default rounds to 2
             )

                       1    2    3    4    5    6    7    8
oral sex (1)        1.00 0.73 0.32 0.13 0.12 0.18 0.16 0.07
pleasing sexual (2) 0.64 1.00 0.35 0.11 0.11 0.15 0.18 0.15
rec_aos_pt_3 (3)    0.24 0.31 1.00 0.28 0.26 0.24 0.29 0.17
rec_aos_1 (4)       0.08 0.06 0.24 1.00 0.44 0.37 0.38 0.29
rec_aos_2 (5)       0.08 0.11 0.22 0.36 1.00 0.33 0.35 0.32
rec_aos_3 (6)       0.14 0.14 0.25 0.24 0.29 1.00 0.56 0.42
rec_aos_4 (7)       0.08 0.02 0.21 0.30 0.31 0.61 1.00 0.51
rec_aos_5 (8)       0.08 0.10 0.26 0.25 0.25 0.46 0.57 1.00
attr(,"note")
[1] "Upper triangle = Male, Lower triangle = Female"
attr(,"class")
[1] "pub_cors_mat" "matrix"       "array"

title: Home
text:
About
I am a Ph.D. student in the Evolution and Society area of the Department of Psychology at the University of New Mexico. My research focuses on the evolutionary and developmental foundations of human social relationships and sexual behavior. Much of my work examines cross-sex friendships, relationship dynamics, and the developmental pathways that shape variation in sexual behavior.
I am broadly interested in integrating multiple methodological approaches—including genetically informed designs, longitudinal and intensive longitudinal data, and hormonal measures—to better understand how biological and environmental processes jointly influence human social and mating behavior.

title: Home
text:
Research
More information about my current research projects and publications can be found on the Research page.

title: Home
text:
Code
Much of my work involves statistical modeling, data cleaning, and reproducible research workflows in R (and sometimes Python).
After many long and painful hours of coding, I decide to start to moving reusable functions into an R package stored on Github. codechest is my personal R package with many convenience functions.
I can’t promise all of the functions work perfectly, but some of them may be useful to others. More details on the Code page.
